items.title: Chrome V8 Zero-Day CVE-2026-11645 Exploited in the Wild
items.desc: CVSS 8.8 out-of-bounds memory access in V8 actively exploited. Patch Chrome 149.0.7827.103 now.
items.summary: Google patched 74 Chrome vulnerabilities including CVE-2026-11645 (CVSS 8.8), an out-of-bounds read/write in V8 that allows arbitrary code execution inside the sandbox via a crafted HTML page. Active exploitation in the wild confirmed. This is the 5th Chrome zero-day exploited in 2026. Researcher '303f06e3' credited with discovery.
items.importance: Chrome is the most targeted browser. An actively exploited V8 zero-day means every unpatched Chrome user is a potential victim. The sandbox escape is the critical question — if paired with a sandbox escape, this is full system compromise from a single webpage visit.
items.noteworthy: Update Chrome to 149.0.7827.103+ immediately. Check Chromium-based browsers (Edge, Brave, Opera) for their own patches. Enable auto-updates. Consider using a separate browser profile for high-risk browsing.

items.title: Miasma Worm Hits 73 Microsoft GitHub Repos — Supply Chain Attack
items.desc: Self-replicating worm compromised 73 Microsoft repos across Azure, Microsoft, MicrosoftDocs, and Azure-Samples. GitHub disabled access.
items.summary: The Miasma self-replicating supply chain worm hit 73 Microsoft GitHub repositories across four organizations: Azure, Azure-Samples, Microsoft, and MicrosoftDocs. GitHub disabled access to affected repos. Miasma is a variant of the Mini Shai-Hulud worm publicly released in mid-May 2026. Microsoft has since restored some repos while keeping others offline as the investigation continues. The worm injects an information stealer into code.
items.importance: Microsoft's own GitHub repos being compromised by a self-replicating worm is a watershed moment for supply chain security. If Microsoft can't protect its own open-source infrastructure, the trust model for all GitHub-hosted software is called into question. The worm's self-replicating nature means it spreads autonomously.
items.noteworthy: Audit any dependencies pulled from Microsoft GitHub repos in the last 30 days. Pin dependency versions. Enable GitHub Dependabot alerts. Monitor for Miasma indicators of compromise. Consider mirroring critical dependencies to private registries.

items.title: IronWorm + Miasma Variant Hit npm: 50+ Packages Compromised
items.desc: Two supply chain worms hit npm simultaneously — IronWorm and a Miasma variant — distributing Rust-based infostealer across 50+ packages.
items.summary: JFrog reported multiple supply chain attacks hitting npm, with threat actors using malicious and poisoned versions of over 50 legitimate packages to distribute a Rust-based information stealer and a self-spreading worm. The Miasma worm variant targets npm packages, while IronWorm is a separate campaign. Both scrape developer secrets: environment variables, SSH keys, cloud tokens, and API credentials.
items.importance: npm is the largest package registry in the world. Two concurrent worm campaigns targeting it means the ecosystem is under active, automated attack. The self-replicating nature of these worms means they spread faster than manual takedowns can contain them.
items.noteworthy: Audit npm dependencies for any packages matching Miasma/IronWorm indicators. Pin all dependency versions. Consider using npm audit and Socket.dev for real-time supply chain monitoring. Rotate any credentials that may have been exposed.

items.title: One-Character Linux Kernel Flaw: Local Root + Container Escape (CVE-2026-23111)
items.desc: Use-after-free in nf_tables lets unprivileged users escalate to root and break out of containers. Working exploit published.
items.summary: Exodus Intelligence published a working exploit for CVE-2026-23111, a use-after-free in the Linux kernel's nf_tables packet-filtering code. A single-character bug lets an unprivileged local user escalate to root and break out of containers. The flaw was patched upstream on February 5, 2026, but many distros may not have deployed the fix. Working exploit code is now public.
items.importance: Local root + container escape from a single-character bug is the kind of vulnerability that powers privilege escalation chains. If you run multi-tenant containers or have untrusted users on Linux systems, this is a direct threat. The public exploit means script kiddies can weaponize it.
items.noteworthy: Verify your kernel is patched beyond February 5, 2026. Check distro kernel versions against the fix commit. For container hosts, ensure seccomp profiles and AppArmor/SELinux are enforced. The exploit requires local access — harden SSH and user authentication.

items.title: FROST Attack: Websites Track Your Apps via SSD Timing — No Permissions Needed
items.desc: Novel side-channel attack uses JavaScript + OPFS SSD timing to identify which websites and apps you use at 88.95% F1 accuracy.
items.summary: Researchers disclosed FROST, a novel side-channel attack that uses JavaScript and the Origin Private File System (OPFS) to time SSD operations. By measuring drive contention, a malicious website can determine which other sites and apps you have open — with 88.95% F1 accuracy. No native code, no extensions, and no permission prompts required. The attack works across browsers because it exploits the SSD's physical characteristics, not a browser bug.
items.importance: This is a fundamental privacy leak that no browser setting can fix. It exploits the physics of SSDs, not a software bug. Every modern browser supports OPFS, making this a cross-browser tracking vector that ad blockers and privacy extensions cannot mitigate.
items.noteworthy: No patch available — this is a hardware-level side channel. Mitigations include disabling OPFS in browser flags (may break web apps), using RAM-backed temp storage, or switching to a system without SSDs. For sensitive work, consider separate physical machines.

items.title: AI Agent Uncovers 21 Zero-Days in FFmpeg; Chrome Patches Record 429 Bugs
items.desc: Autonomous AI agent finds 21 zero-days in FFmpeg. Same week, Chrome ships record 429 patches in a single release.
items.summary: Two events within days: depthfirst's autonomous AI agent discovered 21 previously unknown vulnerabilities in FFmpeg, the media library used in almost everything that touches video. The same week, Google shipped Chrome 149 with patches for 429 security bugs — the most ever in a single Chrome release. The FFmpeg findings include bugs latent for 15-20 years, with a working RCE PoC developed. Chrome's 429 patches span V8, GPU, networking, and extensions.
items.importance: AI-powered vulnerability discovery is no longer theoretical — it's producing results at scale. 21 zero-days in a single library, found autonomously, at 10% of traditional cost. Chrome's 429-bug release signals that the vulnerability surface is expanding faster than manual review can keep up.
items.noteworthy: Patch FFmpeg to latest version. Sandbox all media processing. Update Chrome to 149.0.7827.103+. The AI vulnerability discovery trend means more CVEs are coming — prepare for increased patch velocity.

items.title: ChatGPT Lockdown Mode: OpenAI's Answer to Prompt Injection Data Theft
items.desc: OpenAI rolls out Lockdown Mode for ChatGPT personal accounts, restricting tools that could enable data exfiltration via prompt injection.
items.summary: OpenAI began rolling out Lockdown Mode to ChatGPT for eligible personal accounts. The feature restricts tools that could be used for data exfiltration via prompt injection attacks — limiting code interpreter, file uploads, and web browsing capabilities when sensitive data is present. OpenAI acknowledges Lockdown Mode does not prevent all prompt injection effects — a malicious instruction hidden in an uploaded file could still affect ChatGPT's behavior and cause incorrect answers.
items.importance: This is the first major platform-level response to prompt injection as a data exfiltration vector. For anyone handling sensitive data through AI assistants, Lockdown Mode is a necessary but insufficient mitigation. The admission that it doesn't fully prevent prompt injection effects is honest but concerning.
items.noteworthy: Enable Lockdown Mode in ChatGPT settings if handling sensitive data. Continue to avoid uploading sensitive documents to AI assistants. Treat AI-generated output as untrusted. This is a mitigation, not a solution — the underlying prompt injection problem remains unsolved.

items.title: Cisco Unified CM RCE (CVE-2026-20230): Exploit Code Goes Public
items.desc: Unauthenticated attacker can write files and escalate to root on Cisco Unified Communications Manager. PoC exploit code now public.
items.summary: Cisco patched CVE-2026-20230 in Unified Communications Manager, which lets an unauthenticated attacker on the network write files to the box and escalate to root. Proof-of-concept exploit code is already public. The vulnerability affects Cisco Unified CM and Unified CM SME. CISA has added it to the KEV catalog alongside Chrome and Arista flaws.
items.importance: Unified CM is the phone system backbone for thousands of enterprises. An unauthenticated file write + root escalation with public exploit code means every unpatched Unified CM is a ticking bomb. Voice infrastructure is often overlooked in patch management.
items.noteworthy: Patch Cisco Unified CM immediately. If patching isn't possible, restrict network access to Unified CM management interfaces. Audit for signs of compromise. CISA KEV listing means federal contractors have 14 days to patch.

items.title: CISA Adds Cisco, Chrome, and Arista Flaws to KEV Catalog
items.desc: Three new vulnerabilities added to CISA's Known Exploited Vulnerabilities catalog: Cisco Unified CM, Chrome V8, and Arista EOS.
items.summary: CISA added three vulnerabilities to its KEV catalog: CVE-2026-20230 (Cisco Unified CM RCE), CVE-2026-11645 (Chrome V8 zero-day), and an Arista EOS flaw. All are under active exploitation. The KEV listing requires federal agencies to patch within 14 days and serves as a priority signal for all organizations.
items.importance: CISA KEV is the de facto priority list for enterprise patch management. Three additions in one batch signals that attackers are actively targeting these vectors. The Arista EOS inclusion is notable — network infrastructure is increasingly in the crosshairs.
items.noteworthy: Check your environment for all three products. Prioritize patching in KEV order. The 14-day federal mandate is a good benchmark for all organizations.

items.title: Smart TVs Turned Into Web-Scraping Proxies for AI via Free Apps
items.desc: Researcher reverse-engineers Bright Data's iOS SDK embedded in free apps, turning smart TVs into residential proxies for AI web scraping.
items.summary: A researcher reverse-engineered the iOS SDK that Bright Data embeds in consumer apps, documenting how it turns devices — including smart TVs — into residential proxies for web scraping. Free apps on smart TVs quietly route traffic through the device, using the home's IP address to scrape websites for AI training data. The device owner is unaware their bandwidth and IP are being used as a proxy node.
items.importance: Your smart TV is now a proxy server for AI companies. This is the next evolution of the 'free app monetization' model — instead of ads, your device's IP address is the product. For home lab operators, this means your home IP could be flagged for scraping activity you didn't initiate.
items.noteworthy: Audit smart TV apps. Remove any free apps that don't need network access. Monitor your home IP for unusual outbound traffic patterns. Consider using a VPN at the router level to prevent IP-based tracking. Block unknown outbound connections from IoT VLAN.

items.title: Unpatched Windows Search URI Leaks NTLMv2 Hashes
items.desc: Windows Search URI handler can be abused to leak NTLMv2 hashes to remote attackers. No patch available.
items.summary: Researchers disclosed an unpatched issue in Windows Search URI handling that can be exploited to disclose a user's NTLMv2 hashes. An attacker can trigger the leak by getting a user to click a specially crafted link, which causes Windows to authenticate to a remote SMB share, sending the user's NTLMv2 hash. The hash can then be cracked offline or used in relay attacks. No patch is currently available from Microsoft.
items.importance: NTLMv2 hash theft is a classic attack vector that keeps resurfacing. The hash can be cracked offline or relayed to authenticate as the user. With no patch available, the only mitigation is blocking outbound SMB traffic and educating users about suspicious links.
items.noteworthy: Block outbound SMB (port 445) at the firewall. Disable NTLM authentication where possible via Group Policy. Educate users not to click suspicious links. Monitor for unexpected SMB outbound connections. This is unpatched — assume all Windows versions are vulnerable.

items.title: HTTP/2 Bomb: Remote DoS on NGINX, Apache, IIS, Envoy & Cloudflare
items.desc: New HTTP/2 vulnerability enables remote denial-of-service across major web servers and reverse proxies.
items.summary: Researchers discovered a remote denial-of-service exploit affecting multiple HTTP/2 implementations including NGINX, Apache HTTP Server, IIS, Envoy, and Cloudflare. The 'HTTP/2 Bomb' exploits how different servers handle HTTP/2 stream multiplexing, causing resource exhaustion with minimal bandwidth. The attack can take down servers with a single connection.
items.importance: HTTP/2 is the backbone of modern web traffic. A DoS that works across every major web server and CDN is a systemic vulnerability. For anyone running web infrastructure, this is a must-patch across the entire stack.
items.noteworthy: Update NGINX, Apache, IIS, and Envoy to latest versions. Check Cloudflare status for their mitigation. Implement rate limiting and connection limits as defense-in-depth. Monitor for unusual HTTP/2 connection patterns.

items.title: Market: BTC $65.7K (+1.9%). ETH $1,718. SOL $71.1.
items.desc: Crypto markets show modest recovery. BTC holds above $65K. SOL leads with +3.2%.
items.summary: Crypto markets showed a modest recovery with BTC at $65,690 (+1.9% 24h), ETH at $1,718 (+2.2%), and SOL at $71.13 (+3.2%). The recovery follows last week's stabilization above $64K. BTC market cap stands at $1.29T. The $70K floor remains broken but $65K is holding as a new support level. SOL is showing relative strength, decoupling from BTC's trajectory.
items.importance: The market is in a consolidation phase after the structural correction. $65K is holding as support but $70K remains the key resistance. SOL's outperformance suggests capital rotation within crypto. The macro environment (tariffs, AI regulation) remains the dominant driver.
items.noteworthy: Watch $65K BTC support — a break below signals retest of $58K. SOL at $71 is showing strength but still below pre-crash levels. No clear reversal signal yet. Hold cash, wait for confirmation.

items.title: Splunk Enterprise RCE: Unauthenticated Code Execution (CVE-2026-20253)
items.desc: CVSS 9.8 flaw in Splunk Enterprise lets unauthenticated attackers create/truncate arbitrary files via PostgreSQL sidecar, leading to RCE.
items.summary: Splunk patched CVE-2026-20253, a critical 9.8 vulnerability in Splunk Enterprise below 10.2.4 and 10.0.7. The PostgreSQL sidecar service endpoint lacks authentication controls entirely. watchTowr Labs published full exploitation details.
items.importance: Splunk is the nervous system of most enterprise SOC operations. An unauthenticated RCE on the SIEM means attackers can blind defenders before they know there's a problem.
items.noteworthy: Patch Splunk Enterprise to 10.0.7+ or 10.2.4+. Restrict network access to PostgreSQL sidecar endpoint. Audit Splunk logs for unauthorized file operations.

items.title: US Orders Anthropic to Suspend Fable 5 & Mythos 5 for Foreign Nationals
items.desc: US government orders Anthropic to cut off foreign national access to its most advanced models, citing national security concerns over jailbreaking.
items.summary: Anthropic received a US government order to suspend all access to Claude Fable 5 and Mythos 5 for foreign nationals. The government claimed a jailbreak method was discovered. Anthropic called it a misunderstanding and is working to restore access.
items.importance: First time the US has directly intervened to restrict AI model access on national security grounds. Precedent is set: government can order an AI company to suspend specific models overnight.
items.noteworthy: If using Claude Fable 5 or Mythos 5 via API, expect interruptions. Consider fallback models. This is likely the first of many export-control actions targeting frontier AI.

items.title: 400+ Arch Linux AUR Packages Hijacked: Infostealer + eBPF Rootkit
items.desc: Attackers took over 400+ AUR packages, injecting a Rust infostealer and eBPF rootkit into build scripts.
items.summary: Over 400 AUR packages hijacked by attackers who adopted abandoned packages and rewrote build scripts. Payload: Rust infostealer + eBPF rootkit. Official Arch repos not affected.
items.importance: eBPF rootkits are nearly undetectable by conventional means. If you built any AUR package since June 11, assume compromise.
items.noteworthy: Check all AUR packages installed/updated since June 11. If compromised, wipe and rebuild. Consider switching to official repos only for critical systems.

items.title: Agentjacking: AI Coding Agents Tricked Into Running Malicious Code
items.desc: New attack class injects malicious instructions via Sentry error reports, which AI coding agents interpret as legitimate fixes and execute.
items.summary: Tenet Security disclosed Agentjacking — exploiting Sentry's event ingestion (accepts arbitrary payloads) and AI coding agents that consume Sentry data as trusted system output. Affects Claude Code, Cursor, and similar agents.
items.importance: First systematic attack targeting AI coding agents via their monitoring pipeline. Every developer using Claude Code or Cursor with Sentry integration is potentially exposed.
items.noteworthy: Audit Sentry DSN exposure. Implement human-in-the-loop review for AI-suggested code changes based on external data. Rotate credentials processed by AI agents with Sentry access.

items.title: Velvet Ant: China-Linked APT Backdoored Linux Login for Nearly a Decade
items.desc: China-nexus group replaced PAM and OpenSSH binaries with backdoored versions. Active since 2016.
items.summary: Sygnia tracks Velvet Ant, a China-nexus group that spent close to a decade hidden inside the Linux login system. Replaced PAM and OpenSSH with backdoored versions that logged credentials and accepted master passwords. Nine backdoor versions found.
items.importance: A decade of undetected access via compromised login binaries. If the login system is compromised, every credential that passed through it is assumed leaked.
items.noteworthy: Verify PAM and OpenSSH binary integrity using package manager checksums. Check for unexpected PAM modules. Consider deploying AIDE or Tripwire for file integrity monitoring.

items.title: 21 Zero-Days in FFmpeg: Autonomous Security Agent Finds Decade-Old Bugs
items.desc: depthfirst's autonomous security agent discovered 21 zero-days in FFmpeg, some latent for 15-20 years, including RCE PoC.
items.summary: depthfirst's autonomous security agent discovered 21 zero-day vulnerabilities in FFmpeg. Several latent for 15-20 years. Working RCE PoC developed. Cost: $1K vs traditional $10K.
items.importance: Watershed moment for AI-powered security research. 21 zero-days found autonomously at 10% of traditional cost. If you process multimedia, FFmpeg is in your stack.
items.noteworthy: Patch FFmpeg immediately. Sandbox all FFmpeg processing. Audit any system processing user-uploaded media.

items.title: GreatXML: New BitLocker Bypass via Windows Recovery Partition
items.desc: Researcher releases BitLocker bypass that drops XML files into the recovery partition to spawn an unrestricted shell.
items.summary: GreatXML bypass discovered accidentally in 4 hours. Copies XML files to recovery partition, boots WinRE, spawns unrestricted shell. Anyone who ran Defender Offline Scan is automatically vulnerable.
items.importance: BitLocker is theater for any attacker with physical access. 4-hour accidental discovery means your disk encryption is not the protection you think it is.
items.noteworthy: Enable BitLocker with TPM+PIN. Consider disabling WinRE on high-security endpoints. Pair disk encryption with hardware-level security.

items.title: ShinyHunters Exploits Oracle PeopleSoft Zero-Day (CVE-2026-35273)
items.desc: CVSS 9.8 unauthenticated RCE in Oracle PeopleSoft exploited by ShinyHunters to breach universities. Zero-day for 2 weeks.
items.summary: ShinyHunters exploited CVE-2026-35273 (CVSS 9.8) in Oracle PeopleSoft Enterprise PeopleTools. Active May 27-June 9. Oracle patched June 10. No login or interaction required.
items.importance: PeopleSoft runs the administrative backbone of thousands of universities. 9.8 unauthenticated RCE exploited for 2 weeks before patch. If exposed to internet, assume compromise.
items.noteworthy: Patch PeopleTools 8.61 and 8.62. Restrict PSEMHUB endpoint access. Audit PeopleSoft logs for unusual activity May 27-June 10.

items.title: The Gentlemen Ransomware: 478 Victims, Worm-Like Spread, AI-Powered
items.desc: PRODAFT analysis reveals The Gentlemen group operates independently, uses AI for ransomware development, 478 victims.
items.summary: Russian-speaking group led by LARVA-368. Started as LockBit/Qilin/Medusa affiliate, transitioned to independent in July 2025. Uses AI for ransomware dev and post-exploitation. Worm-like lateral spread.
items.importance: AI-assisted ransomware development is confirmed operational. Worm-like spread means flat networks are death traps.
items.noteworthy: Ensure network segmentation. Disable SMB between VLANs. Verify backup integrity — double extortion means encryption + data theft.

items.title: LangGraph RCE Chain: SQL Injection Leads to Full Agent Takeover
items.desc: Three patched flaws in LangGraph chain together for unauthenticated RCE on self-hosted AI agent servers.
items.summary: Check Point disclosed three LangGraph vulnerabilities: CVE-2025-67644 (SQL injection in SQLite checkpoint), CVE-2026-28277 (unsafe msgpack deserialization). Chained for full RCE on agent servers.
items.importance: LangGraph is the default framework for production AI agent deployments. RCE against agent infrastructure is a direct hit on the most sensitive layer.
items.noteworthy: Update langgraph to 0.4.1+ and langgraph-checkpoint-sqlite to 3.0.1+. Restrict network access to LangGraph servers.

items.title: npm Install Scripts Disabled by Default: GitHub's Supply Chain Fix
items.desc: GitHub announces npm v12 will disable install scripts by default — the single largest code-execution surface in the npm ecosystem.
items.summary: npm 12 disables install scripts by default. GitHub calls install-time lifecycle scripts the 'single largest code-execution surface in the npm ecosystem.' Release scheduled next month.
items.importance: Most impactful supply chain security improvement npm has ever made. Install scripts have been the primary vector for npm-based supply chain attacks for years.
items.noteworthy: Audit projects for postinstall/preinstall script dependencies. Test with '--ignore-scripts' flag now to identify breakage before npm 12 lands.

items.title: OceanLotus Targets Vietnam Investors with SPECTRALVIPER via FireAnt
items.desc: Vietnam-aligned OceanLotus deploys SPECTRALVIPER backdoor via FireAnt Metakit supply chain attack targeting stock investors.
items.summary: OceanLotus attributed to two campaigns: espionage against Vietnamese infrastructure firm (mid-2024 to Feb 2026) and supply chain attack on FireAnt Metakit targeting stock investors (Oct 2025 to Mar 2026). SPECTRALVIPER backdoor deployed.
items.importance: Local supply chain attack hitting your region directly. Vietnamese stock investors are specifically targeted.
items.noteworthy: Audit FireAnt/Metakit installations. Check for SPECTRALVIPER IoCs. Warn contacts using domestic trading platforms.
